FT_IC1.evidence: Uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10 years; U.S. ABCD study confirmed with ABCD 5.0 release data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; linear mixed-effects models examining association between screen time and cognitive function; mediation analysis of putamen volume.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Acta Psychologica 265 (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as multi-device composite: "digital devices, including television, videos, video games, texting, and video chat" with no per-device breakdown isolating phone or social media. Per criteria: multi-device aggregated time with no per-modality isolation does not qualify.

notes: Paper studies general screen time (TV, video games, videos, texting, video chat) without isolating phone or social media use as a distinct variable. Fails on FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: From the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study at two-year follow-up, 1,036 participants (Mage = 12.0); U.S. ABCD study confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM) using machine-learning approach with whole-brain functional connectivity fMRI data; 10-fold cross-validation reported.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Behavioral Addictions 15(1) (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: The paper studies problematic gaming severity (measured via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire [VGAQ], a 6-item scale assessing gaming addiction symptoms), not smartphone or social media use. Gaming is console/PC-based behavioral addiction outcome, not a phone or SM exposure variable. Per criteria: "console/PC-gaming-only...does NOT qualify" and "problematic/addictive SM scales..." measure addiction, not the modality itself. No qualifying smartphone/SM use variable exists.

notes: Paper examines neural predictors of problematic gaming (behavioral addiction outcome), not smartphone or social media use as exposure. Gaming disorder is not a qualifying SM/phone variable per criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Retrospective cohort study using ABCD data; 551 participants at T1 baseline, 46.5% girls, 9-10 years at baseline; U.S. ABCD study confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal design with linear mixed models examining associations between cyberbullying victimization (T1), brain measures (T1 and T2), and mental health outcomes (T2); mediation analyses conducted.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Medical Internet Research 28(1) (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: The paper measures cyberbullying victimization via questionnaire: "In the past 12 months, have you been cyberbullied..." as the primary independent variable. Per criteria: "Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify." No independent smartphone use or social media use variable is measured; cyberbullying victimization is the only exposure construct.

notes: Paper examines cyberbullying victimization as independent variable (without separately measured SM/phone use) predicting neural and mental health outcomes. Cyberbullying alone does not meet FT-IC2 requirement.
